    Case Study II:

    Redevelopment of Yue Man Square in Kwun Tong

    The facade of Yue Man Square in Kwun Tong. Photo: Drawn by author

    The facade of Yue Man Square in Kwun Tong. Photo: Drawn by author

    Within the run-down Yue Man Square neighborhood of Kwun Tong Town Centre, crumbling and blackened buildings once framed a bustling cluster of small businesses. Food stalls, clothing vendors, jewelry shops and watch repair services operated out of modest shacks, serving generations of nearby residents.

    Locals could find nearly everything they needed within walking distance — from daily necessities to a comforting bowl of warm tofu dessert — making the area a vibrant community hub despite its aging façade. 

    Image captions

      Today, most of the businesses and residents are gone, forced to make way for the final phase of the Kwun Tong Town Centre redevelopment project, which cleared the area by the end of February this year.

      The town centre redevelopment, which affected about 1,653 property interests and 3,139 people, will provide 1,700 flats, 357,580 square feet of retail space, a two-level public transport interchange, a hawker bazaar and a refuse collecting point upon completion.

      Although it was approved by the Town Planning Board in 2009, the HK$30 billion redevelopment project is not scheduled for completion until 2026.

      Most of the stores at Yue Man Square have been cleared to make way for the Kwun Tong Town Centre Redevelopment project. Photo: Google Earth

      Most of the stores at Yue Man Square have been cleared to make way for the Kwun Tong Town Centre Redevelopment project. Photo: Google Earth

      Most of the stores at Yue Man Square have been cleared to make way for the Kwun Tong Town Centre Redevelopment project. Photo: Author

      Most of the stores at Yue Man Square have been cleared to make way for the Kwun Tong Town Centre Redevelopment project. Photo: Author

      A small exhibition in Yue Man Square with posts telling the history and development of the place over the years. Photo: Author

      A small exhibition in Yue Man Square with posts telling the history and development of the place over the years. Photo: Author

      Artist's impression of Yue Man Square. Photo: Author

      Artist's impression of Yue Man Square. Photo: Author

      Artist's impression of Yue Man Square. Photo: Author

      Artist's impression of Yue Man Square. Photo: Author

      Grand Central, one of the private residential projects in the future redeveloped Kwun Tong. Photo: Author

      Grand Central, one of the private residential projects in the future redeveloped Kwun Tong. Photo: Author

      The redevelopment history of Kwun Tong Town Centre

      Image captions

        What has stalled the redevelopment of Yue Man Square?

        1. Prolonged property acquisition

        Kwun Tong District Councillor Patrick Lai Shu-ho said the slow pace of the Yue Man Square redevelopment was mainly due to the prolonged acquisition of properties in the area.

        “The acquisition involved more than 1,600 property interests, requiring substantial manpower and the handling of a large volume of conveyancing documents,” Lai said.

        “It is an incredibly meticulous task.”

        As of early last year, about 98% of the roughly 100 property interests in Yue Man Square had yet to be successfully acquired, according to the Urban Renewal Authority (URA). 

        Image captions

          1.1 Cases of unidentifiable ownership and adverse possession

          Lai said the acquisition process had been slowed by cases involving unidentifiable ownership and adverse possession — a situation in which an individual occupies land without legal title for a prolonged period and may ultimately claim ownership over the original legal owner.

          In a 2007's freezing survey, the URA identified 100 unregistered properties in Yue Man Square involving both unidentifiable ownership and adverse possession. These cases were unevenly distributed across government lots, fixed-pitch licensed hawker areas and public passages within private buildings.

          The authority previously acknowledged that handling such cases had become increasingly challenging in recent years, as it was not authorized to conduct a second freezing survey to determine the latest occupancy status of the properties.

          “If the Lands Department had invoked the Land Resumption Ordinance earlier, particularly in cases involving absentee stall owners, the acquisition process could have been expedited,” he said.

          “However, many business and residential occupants had yet to reach agreement with the authority on compensation, so invoking the ordinance could have backfired.”

          He suggested that the Lands Registry — the government department responsible for land registration — should regularly maintain records of the distribution of both public and private ownership in future designated renewal areas to help expedite acquisitions.

          Kwun Tong District Councillor Chan Wah-yu said the URA should refine its existing acquisition mechanism in future designated renewal areas to help speed up the process.

          “Any such acquisition mechanism should be grounded in in-depth consultations with key stakeholders, including business operators and residents, and adopt a bottom-up approach,” Chan said.

          Image captions

            1.2 Acquisition process carried out in disorderly manner

            Lai said the prolonged acquisition in Yue Man Square was due to the disorderly implementation of the Kwun Tong Town Centre project in phases.

            The three-phase project comprises five development areas, with Development Area 1 forming the first phase; Development Areas 2 and 3 the second phase; and Development Areas 4 and 5 the third.

            The five redevelopment areas under the Kwun Tong Town Centre Redevelopment Project. Source: Google Map, modified by author in accordance to the source by the Urban Renewal Authority

            The five redevelopment areas under the Kwun Tong Town Centre Redevelopment Project. Source: Google Map, modified by author in accordance to the source by the Urban Renewal Authority

            However, he explained that the URA had suggested developing the project in irregular phases and acquiring all property interests in one go would be more time-efficient.

            “But the authority has overlooked a range of concerns, including traffic, development density and the preservation of local characteristics, which could also affect the acquisition process,” he said.

            Lai said this was partly why the acquisition of Yue Man Square, which falls under Development Area 5, had fallen significantly behind schedule.

            In March 2017, the URA announced that Development Areas 4 and 5 would be separated into two individual redevelopment phases in light of the slow acquisition progress in Yue Man Square.

            “The acquisition could have been faster if it had been conducted in sequence instead.”

            He urged the URA to regularly update the district council on the progress of the acquisition and to report any problems encountered, so that both parties could work together to reach mutually agreed solutions.

            Image captions

              2. Cumbersome negotiations with stall operators

              2.1 Dissatisfaction over compensation arrangements

              Nearly 90 per cent of the 116 unauthorised stall operators at Yue Man Square who accepted a one-off compensation package totalling HK$100 million from the URA had relocated by the end of February this year.

              Among those who left reluctantly was 62-year-old Mr Lai, who had operated a newspaper stall in Yue Man Square for more than half a century.

              Lai said he was dissatisfied with the basic allowance, one of the compensation packages offered to stall owners at Yue Man Square.

              Image captions

                “The authority considers my stall to be facing inward toward the alley rather than outward toward Yue Man Square,” Lai said.

                “As a result, I received only half the compensation granted to the so-called ‘qualified’ stalls facing Yue Man Square,” he said.

                According to the URA, stalls facing outward towards to Yue Man Square were compensated at a basic allowance of HKD$2,660 per square foot, while those facing inward toward the alley receieved HKD$1,300 per square foot.

                He said the URA needed to set out clear criteria for what constituted “alley-facing” and “Yue Man Square-facing” stalls to ensure fair compensation.

                “The URA has never explained why my stall’s compensation was calculated based on the alley-facing rate,” he said.

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                In addition to the basic allowance, business operators were eligible for compensation based on the length of their operation, as well as relocation allowances and arrangements. An additional allowance was also offered to those who accepted the terms before the offer period expired at the end of February this year.

                Wu Chi-wai, a Democratic Party lawmaker and non-official member of the Urban Renewal Authority board, said some remaining stall owners were unhappy with the relocation allowances offered if their businesses move to a rebuilt mall in future.

                Wu explained that the owners were concerned the relocation allowance would not be enough to offset their new stores’ operating costs in the short term.

                “They hoped the allowance would better reflect market rates,” Wu said.

                New stores in the rebuilt mall would enjoy a concessionary rent set at about half the market rate for three years. However, their owners would receive HK$150,000 less in relocation allowance.

                “Even with the future mall’s rent cut by half, operating costs for the new stores could still be five to ten times higher than those in Yue Man Square,” he said.

                Fewer than ten business operators had accepted the offer to relocate to the nearby rebuilt market by the end of February this year.

                Chan said the authority should extend the concessionary rental period beyond three years, rather than “drag its heels” over the allowance payouts.

                “At the end of the day, operators just want their businesses to be sustainable,” Chan said. “If that issue is settled, the size of the allowance won’t matter much.”

                Image captions

                  2.2 Fear of losing old customers, key revenue source

                  Yuen Chi-yan, founder of the concern group Living in Kwun Tong, said many operators at Yue Man Square were reluctant to leave their original stalls for fear of losing long-time customers.

                  “Even if the government has a comprehensive relocation plan that guarantees operators a thriving business, it would take at least two to four years to complete,” Yuen said.

                  Yuen suggested the government set up a temporary outdoor bazaar near the original site for stall owners before the redevelopment is completed.

                  He said the existing Tung Yan Street Temporary Hawker Bazaar, which has rehoused hawkers from Mut Wah Street affected by the redevelopment since 2014, was a poor example of temporary resettlement as it is located indoors and far from major consumer traffic.

                  Image captions

                    2.3 Concern over Yue Man Square relocation

                    The permanent hawker bazaar in Development Area 3 of the redevelopment project—which will rehouse Yue Man Square street merchants who accept the relocation offer—is expected to be completed in 2021.

                    Nevertheless, operators have raised concerns about the new bazaar’s accessibility, which could hurt their business.

                    The 'dungeon' bazaar

                    According to the URA’s plan, the bazaar will be located on the lower-ground floor of a mixed-use complex on the redeveloped site.

                    Cross section of the Permanent Hawker Bazaar. Source: MingPao

                    Yuen called the bazaar’s location unrealistic.

                    “Passengers must take the escalator two floors down to reach it—the operators’ business will surely suffer,” he said.

                    It is not the first time the URA has resettled stall owners’ businesses to remote areas. From 1989 to 2005, 18 open-air food stalls in Mong Kok were relocated far from the district centre to make way for the new Langham Place shopping centre, forcing most of them to close.

                    Langham Place the shopping centre in Mongkok district. Photo: Baycrest/WikiCommons

                    Langham Place the shopping centre in Mongkok district. Photo: Baycrest/WikiCommons

                    Narrow aisles in bazaar 

                    Yuen also feared that the design of the new bazaar would be following in the footsteps of that of Tung Yan Street Temporary Hawker Bazaar in the same district, where each aisle is only five feet wide.

                    "Operators in the Tung Yan Street Bazaar have been complaining about customers not staying long because of the narrow space there," said Yuen. 

                    Ms Li, who has been operating her clothing store in the Tung Yan Street Temporary Bazaar for two years, said it was disappointing to see the issue of narrow aisles happening in the future permanent bazaar again. 

                    "I requested stretching the aisle to as wide as ten feet - but the idea fell on deaf ears," said Ms Li. 

                    "It is not too much to ask for because you need more space so that customers can walk comfortably and have the patience to check out our stalls."

                    Image captions

                      Bad ventilation 

                      Some hawkers in the Temporary Hawker Bazaar described its ventilation as bad as a “cooking steamer,” which could deter customers.

                      Wu said the URA should prioritize existing operators’ needs rather than simply finding a building to resettle them.

                      “Once stalls are resettled in a building, they lose the capacity for street-level economic activity,” Wu said.

                      Yuen said the URA should improve communication with the Kwun Tong District Council and the business operators, and to ensure their views would be fully taken into consideration in the design drafting of the new bazaar. 

                      He also urged the URA to address space issues in future resettlement buildings to attract more owners to relocate their businesses during redevelopments.

                      "Both the government and the URA have a misconception that the gross floor area of a building defines its saleable space," he said. "Which is not the case."

                      He cited the Kwun Tong Government Offices as an example, where government departments sit atop a shopping mall that occupies most of the gross floor area.

                      “The design has incredibly poor accessibility to the government offices, leaving them as a by-product with little saleable area—a sign of regression,” he said.

                      Image captions

                        3. URA’s early capital shortfall

                        Yuen said the URA’s lack of capital in its early days was another factor behind Kwun Tong’s slow redevelopment pace.

                        “The URA is, in fact, a self-financing company, even though it has never branded itself as one,” Yuen said.

                        “The land premium exemption and initial government subsidy of HK$1 million granted to the URA are not enough to sustain its operations,” he said.

                        As a result, the URA prioritized collaborating with property developers on costly projects to boost revenue, rather than public redevelopments—which slowed the pace of the latter—he explained.

                        Image captions

                          Of the URA’s 76 redevelopment projects, 31 currently involve collaboration with property developers, according to the authority.

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority , image

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority , image

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority , image

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          Source: The Urban Renewal Authority

                          “This is also how the authority gathers enough funds to compensate affected residents,” he said.

                          Yuen cited the old North Point Estate and Wong Chuk Hang Estate as examples that were turned into private residential projects under URA-private sector collaboration, rather than public-interest infrastructure.

                          “This is a persistent issue in Hong Kong’s land development—and it’s not limited to the URA’s projects,” Yuen said.

                          He said the government should establish an annual subsidy mechanism for the URA to accelerate Kwun Tong’s redevelopment, rather than relying on revenue from private projects.

                          “The URA needs to re-examine whether it is truly applying a ‘people first, district-based’ approach to its existing redevelopment projects,” he said.
